_source: TH_EXPLORE_IT_DATA.json (authoritative structured data). Real accessions, per-item licence + open_access flag. Nothing authored by an assistant.

station.tagline: They cut her off from the world sixty years ago. She is still standing in the doorway.

museum_objects.title: First Cuban Peso Notes (Banco Español de La Habana)
museum_objects.description: The earliest paper peso notes issued in Cuba, by the Spanish Bank of Havana.
museum_objects.significance: Marisol's very first appearance was as a colonial bank's promise — long before anyone imagined she'd end up isolated from the entire global financial system.
museum_objects.plain_language: The very first paper peso notes issued in Cuba, printed by a Spanish colonial bank in 1857.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

museum_objects.title: Castillo de la Real Fuerza
museum_objects.description: A historic Spanish colonial fortress in Havana, symbol of the wealth and defense of Spain's Caribbean trade.
museum_objects.significance: Long before Marisol existed, this fortress was already guarding the gold and silver that would define Cuba's colonial economy.
museum_objects.plain_language: A centuries-old Spanish fort in Havana that once guarded the colony's treasure.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.rights_note: CITED: the original object (rights unclear/blocked). DISPLAYED: an openly-licensed image of the same kind of object, credited to its own institution. We cite one and show the other, and we say so.

display_alternatives.title: Havana - Castillo de la Real Fuerza.jpg

museum_objects.title: Cuban Convertible Peso (CUC) Note
museum_objects.description: A dual-currency system note created to attract foreign tourism dollars while isolating the domestic peso.
museum_objects.significance: For decades, Marisol had to share her own house with a currency built specifically to keep the outside world at arm's length — and closer to her.
museum_objects.plain_language: A special tourist-only currency Cuba created to run alongside the regular peso for nearly three decades.

resolved.warning: TERTIARY SOURCE — acceptable as a pointer, NOT as TH's evidence. Replace with the primary source it cites.

museum_objects.title: Banco de España Historical Study on Cuban Currency
museum_objects.description: A Spanish central bank historical study analyzing 19th-century Cuban colonial monetary systems.
museum_objects.significance: Even the bank that once controlled her is still writing papers trying to understand how Marisol survived.
museum_objects.plain_language: A detailed research paper by Spain's central bank studying Cuba's old colonial-era money system.

resolved.via: long tail — needs a per-domain licence rule

museum_objects.rights_note: NO OPEN IMAGE FOUND — panel must show text/citation only.

museum_objects.title: Day Zero Currency Unification Coverage
museum_objects.description: News photography documenting Cuba's 2021 unification of its dual-currency system into a single peso.
museum_objects.significance: After six decades of living split between two currencies, Marisol finally became one thing again — and the whole world watched it happen.
museum_objects.plain_language: News coverage of the day in 2021 when Cuba finally merged its two different currencies back into one.

primary_sources.author: U.S. Department of Treasury
primary_sources.description: Legal framework underpinning the decades-long U.S. financial embargo affecting the Cuban peso's global convertibility.
primary_sources.plain_language: The actual U.S. government rules that have cut Cuba off from much of the global financial system for over 60 years.

world_map.map_description: Marisol's peso was born in colonial Havana, and after the 1959 revolution and decades of U.S. embargo, has remained largely isolated from the global financial system.

nodes.label: Havana (Banco Español)
nodes.description: Where the first Cuban peso notes were issued by the Spanish colonial bank in 1857.

nodes.label: Castillo de la Real Fuerza
nodes.description: Historic fortress guarding Havana's colonial-era wealth.

nodes.label: Miami, Florida
nodes.description: Home to the largest Cuban exile community, whose remittances have long been a lifeline for the isolated peso economy.

nodes.label: Moscow
nodes.description: Cuba's key economic patron during the Soviet era, propping up the peso through subsidized trade until 1991.

nodes.label: Washington, D.C.
nodes.description: Source of the decades-long U.S. embargo that has isolated Cuba's currency from most of the global financial system.
